claim_text: In melanoma, anti-CTLA-4 treatment reinvigorates exhausted PD-1+Eomes+CD8 T cells.

runs.response_text:
VERDICT: NEI

The first abstract discusses anti-CTLA-4 treatment in melanoma and mentions T-cell exhaustion and PD-1 expression, but it does not specifically characterize the exhausted T cells as "PD-1+Eomes+CD8" cells or directly state that anti-CTLA-4 reinvigorates this specific subset. The study indicates that anti-CTLA-4 alone leads to T-cell exhaustion and PD-L1 upregulation, and that PD-L1 blockade is needed to reverse exhaustion—suggesting anti-CTLA-4 monotherapy does not reinvigorate exh

VERDICT: SUPPORT  
The first abstract discusses a combination treatment of anti-CTLA-4 and PD-L1 blockade that addresses T-cell exhaustion and enhances immune responses in melanoma, implying that anti-CTLA-4 can indeed reinvigorate exhausted PD-1+CD8 T cells when combined appropriately.

VERDICT: NEI
The abstracts discuss the use of anti-CTLA-4 in melanoma and T-cell exhaustion, but none specifically mention the combined markers PD-1+Eomes+CD8 T cells in relation to anti-CTLA-4 treatment. Abstract 4468861 mentions PD-L1 upregulation and T-cell exhaustion, but not the specific markers in the claim.

VERDICT: NEI

The abstracts do not address the specific mechanism of anti-CTLA-4 reinvigorating exhausted PD-1+Eomes+CD8 T cells in melanoma. While doc_id 4468861 discusses anti-CTLA4 therapy in melanoma and mentions T-cell exhaustion and CD8 T cells, it does not specifically describe reinvigoration of PD-1+Eomes+CD8 T cells by anti-CTLA-4 alone — in fact, it suggests anti-CTLA4 primarily inhibits Tregs and that reversing T-cell exhaustion requires addition of PD-L1 blockade.

VERDICT: NEI
The provided abstracts discuss anti-CTLA4 treatment in melanoma and T-cell exhaustion, but they do not specifically mention the reinvigoration of PD-1+Eomes+CD8 T cells or directly connect anti-CTLA4 to Eomes expression.
